entry.title: Re: SuccessFactors Integrations Beginners Guide- Exploring Learning Management System
entry.content:
Great blog! I can confirm that as of 2026, this approach still works well.

I just wanted to add a couple of notes that may help anyone having trouble obtaining the access token from the initial POST request.

First, make sure you're using your Learning instance URL rather than the BizX URL from the SuccessFactors platform. The easiest way I found to get the correct URL was to log in to the Learning Administration home page, copy your company instance URL from the browser, and append:

/learning/oauth-api/rest/v1/token

Also, be aware that your Company ID in Learning may be different from your BizX Company ID. This is important because you'll need to Base64-encode the Learning Company ID when creating the authorization header.

For example, in our environment the URL uses the Company ID shown on the Learning Admin OAuth Token Server page, followed by:

lms.sapsf.com/learning/admin

This matched the URL displayed in our browser and was the correct instance to use for generating the access token successfully.

Hopefully this saves someone else some troubleshooting time!

entry.title: Portugal Periodic VAT Return and Annexes S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition 2023
entry.content:
Hi all,

I am currently configuring the Portugal Periodic VAT Return and respective annexes in the Fiori environment.

At the moment, our export and import declaration numbers are stored in the following fields:

  • BKPF-XREF1_HD (Export)
  • BKPF-XREF2_HD (Import)

However, in the Fiori application, only the fields XBLNR (Reference) and BKTXT (Document Header Text) are available and displayed.

Currently, our process is executed via transaction PTFO10 to generate the annexes, where we are able to populate:

  • BKPF-XREF1_HD for customers
  • BKPF-XREF2_HD for vendors

Given this, I would like to ask:
Is it possible to include the fields BKPF-XREF1_HD and BKPF-XREF2_HD in the Fiori application for the VAT return/annexes?

Thank you in advance for your support.

Best regards,
Deivid

entry.title: Re: How to Include/Exclude Time Types into Eligbility for...
entry.content:
Hi @Batruska 

MDF can be tricky with eligibility rules when combined with data from Job Info, for example with inactive users or people who started midway through the year so clearly it is not my recommended option. 

I'd say if you're going to design something reliable and of quality that the customer can easily troubleshoot you need that field in Job Info. For the first cycle the customer can simply do a manual load into Job Info, it shouldn't be too complicated or risky if they leave other job info fields untouched with &&NO_OVERWRITE&&

Best,

Xavier

entry.title: Questions on WalkMe In-App Help for SAP Cloud ERP
entry.content:
Hello everyone,

we are currently implementing SAP S/4HANA Cloud, public edition for a customer.

In SAP Cloud ALM we came across the task “Activate SAP-Managed WalkMe Content for End Users”. This seems to offer WalkMe as an optional in-app help for selected processes.

I have two questions regarding this:

  1. If we activate the SAP-managed WalkMe content, will it fully or partially replace the existing standard in-app help, or is it more of an additional layer on top of what is already there?
  2. Is there an official overview of which WalkMe flows are already included in the standard Cloud ERP license (i.e. without requiring any additional licensing such as WalkMe Premium)?

The most recent information I was able to find is this SAP Community post from September 2025: Introducing the WalkMe-based alternative in-app he... - SAP Community

Are there any newer or more detailed resources available on this topic (e.g. SAP Notes, Help Portal articles, official licensing overviews, or project experiences)?

Many thanks in advance.

entry.title: Processing Electronic Invoices in France with SAP Document and Reporting Compliance
entry.content:
France is introducing mandatory electronic invoicing and electronic reporting via an Platform Agreed (plateforme agréée, PA), starting September 2026. This post walks you through how to process electronic invoices using SAP Document and Reporting Compliance as your approved platform (plateforme agréée, PA), covering each step and the corresponding document statuses.

 

Domestic Customer Invoices: Creation

The process begins when you post a customer invoice. SAP Document and Reporting Compliance automatically creates an electronic document under the Domestic Customer Invoice process, with an initial status of eDcoument Created.

At this point, you can preview the XML that will be sent to SAP Document and Reporting Compliance, cloud edition via Display > Display/Preview XML.

From here, you have two options:

  • Display Local XML / Display PDF - the electronic document is sent to the cloud edition, which returns the final XML in local format and PDF visualizations (in both French and English) for your review.   Please note that this integration is asynchronous, so the push integration must be fully configured before use. If any errors occur, they can be reviewed in the Resolve Errors app within SAP Document and Reporting Compliance, cloud edition.
  • Submit - either manually or using a background job (EDOC_BACKGROUND).

 

Domestic Customer Invoices: Submission

Upon submission, the electronic document moves through the following statuses:

  1. Sending to Service Requested
  2. Sent to Service
  3. Acknowledged by Service — if the invoice is received successfully in SAP Document and Reporting Compliance, cloud edition. The status updates to Rejected by Service when the cloud edition is unable to accept the electronic document — for example, when resubmitting a file that has already been acknowledged.
  4. Service Error— if any error occurs while processing the invoice - for example if schematron validation fails, PDF creation fails, etc.

If an error occurs, the details will appear in the application log (Goto > Application Log) and, if the issues relate to faulty invoices, you will find schematron validation results in the electronic document history (Goto > History). Look for the file saved with variant ERROR — this file includes all results (including warnings), so focus only on entries classified as fatal. Once you've fixed the root cause, use the Submit button to resubmit.

▎Note: If the document is stuck at Sending to Service Requested, you are most likely missing SAP Notes or configuration settings.

If no errors occurs, the final XML and PDF are generated, pushed back to ERP and stored as attachment in the electronic document history, and the electronic document status is updated to Accepted by the Service.

▎Note: These files are pushed into ERP — there is no pull mechanism available, so integration must be fully set up beforehand. If this status transition does not occur, check the Resolve Errors app for issues in the mappings, file creation, or the push to ERP.

 

PPF Registration (Status CDAR_200)

After the invoice in local format is created, a registration status CDAR_200 is sent to the Public Invoicing Portal (Portail Public de Facturation, PPF). This registration request is also returned to ERP and stored in the electronic document history.

You can track the PPF status in the PPF Status and PPF Status Description fields in the EDOC_COCKPIT or the Manage Electronic Documents app. After the CDAR_200 registration is created and transmitted, the PPF Status Description updates to PPF Request Generated and, subsequently, to Registered with PPF.

PPF then sends an acknowledgement that is processed through SAP Document and Reporting Compliance, cloud edition and integrated back to ERP, updating the field to PPF Acknowledgement Received.

▎Important: If the PPF acknowledgement is not received (i.e., the PPF Status Description does not change to PPF Acknowledgement Received), neither Peppol transmission to the recipient nor Flow 1 to PPF are initiated.

 

Domestic Customer Invoices: Exchange

Invoice sent to recipient via Peppol Network

After acknowledgement of 200 registration, the document is sent to recipient via Peppol Network. The exchange status is tracked through the electronic document status:

Status

Code

Rejected by Receiver Platform

CDAR_213

Received by Receiver Platform

CDAR_202

Received by Customer    

CDAR_203

Acknowledged by Customer

CDAR_204

▎Note:  Please refer to Domestic Supplier Invoices: Receipt to understand when these CDAR statuses are sent for buyers using SAP Document and Reporting Compliance.

▎Note: If the recipient PA does not receive (acknowledge) the invoice, the status remains Accepted by Service, even if the document has already been transmitted via Peppol Network. To check whether transmission via Peppol Network was initiated, use the app Monitor Documents app (Peppol monitor) in the cloud edition. If any failure occurred, the document is in status Sending failed. Any issues with pushing recipient acknowledgements can also be investigated in the Monitor Documents app.

At this stage, the invoice can be processed from a business perspective.

Depending on buyer responses, the following optional and required statuses may follow:

Status

Code

Required?

Accepted by Customer          

CDAR_205

Optional

Partially Accepted by Customer

CDAR_206

Optional

Disputed by Customer          

CDAR_207

Optional

Suspended by Customer         

CDAR_208

Optional

Completed

CDAR_209

Optional (buyer can mark documents as complete that have been suspended)

Rejected by Customer          

CDAR_210

Mandatory

Payment Sent by Customer      

CDAR_211

Optional

 

Invoice tax data sent to PPF (Flow 1)

After acknowledgement, PPF has up to 1 hour to reject the document. If no rejection is received within that window, the document is deemed accepted. At the earliest point between acceptance, rejection, or the 1-hour timeout, the electronic document is updated and transmission of Flow 1 to PPF is initiated.

The Flow 1 sends tax data to PPF and the exchange status is tracked in the PPF Status and PPF Status Description fields:

  • Flow 1 Data Submitted
  • Flow 1 Submission Failed
  • Flow 1 Data Accepted
  • Flow 1 Data Rejected

▎Note: If Flow 1 fails, reprocessing is not currently supported. Further options are being evaluated.

 

Domestic Supplier Invoices: Receipt

Supplier invoices are received in the SAP business system via Peppol Network through SAP Document and Reporting Compliance, cloud edition, which acts as the approved platform.

Before you begin:

  • Routing rules are mandatory, even if you use only one business system. See the SAP Help documentation (https://help.sap.com/docs/cloud-edition/sap-document-and-reporting-compliance-cloud-edition/managing-routing-rules?locale=en-US) for setup guidance.
  • If errors occur during receipt:
    • Check the app Resolve Errors in the cloud edition. Here you can find invoices that could not be pushed to the business system.
    • If no errors seen in Resolve Errors app, use the Monitor Documents app (Peppol Monitor) in the cloud edition to view detailed logs and reprocess failed documents.

When an electronic invoice is received, SAP Document and Reporting Compliance, cloud edition first validates the document. If validation fails, one of the following CDAR statuses is returned to the buyer depending on which check failed:

  • Routing error (CDAR_221) – This is triggered when the address of invoice recipient is not registered with SAP as PA (Endpoint ID doesn't match PartyLegalEntity ID - SIREN).
  • Inadmissible (CDAR_501) – This is sent if XSD Schema validation fails (syntax error).
  • Rejected by Receiver Platform (CDAR_213) - This is sent if schematron validation fails (semantic error).

If any validation fails, the electronic invoice will appear in the Monitor Documents app (Peppol Monitor) in SAP Document and Reporting Compliance, cloud edition with a status of Rejected.

If all validations pass, the invoice is accepted and CDAR_202 (Received by Receiver Platform) is sent to the buyer.

SAP Document and Reporting Compliance, cloud edition then converts the supplier invoice and generates the PDF visualization, in both French and English. Once successful, the invoice is pushed to the business system and CDAR_203 (Received by Customer) is sent to the buyer.

Any errors related to routing rules or the push to the business system can be investigated and resolved in the Resolve Errors app in SAP Document and Reporting Compliance, cloud edition.

▎Note: In the Monitor Documents app (Peppol Monitor), these invoices appear as Document Pushed in green. This status indicates the document has been pushed for processing after CDAR_203 — it does not confirm that the invoice has been successfully pushed to the business system.

The invoice is created asynchronously in the target business system — SAP ERP, SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition, SAP S/4HANA, and SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, SAP Business Network, or SAP Ariba Invoicing.

For SAP ERP, SAP S/4HANA, and SAP S/4HANA Cloud, the invoice is created as an electronic document under the Domestic Supplier Invoices process, and CDAR_204 (Acknowledged by Customer) is sent to the buyer. In the electronic document history, you can find the original invoice sent by the supplier, the invoice converted to UBL format and PDF visualizations (French and English). 

If the acknowledgement is not processed automatically, users can trigger it manually via EDOC_COCKPIT (or the Manage Electronic Documents app).

SAP Document and Reporting Compliance also offers integration with SAP Invoice Management by OpenText, and supports extensibility for automating invoice posting with other solutions.

For more details on processing supplier invoices, see these SAP Community blog posts:

  • I received an electronic supplier invoice, what now? 
  • Configuring Reason Codes and Action Codes for France B2B Supplier Invoices.

 

Payment Received for Domestic Customer Invoices

For invoices related to services where VAT is due based on payment (as opposed to invoice date), sellers must report a Payment Received status (CDAR_212).

When a customer invoice is cleared, SAP Document and Reporting Compliance automatically creates an electronic document under the Domestic Customer Payment process. A few things to note:

  • If a single payment clears multiple invoices, one electronic document is created per cleared invoice.
  • For partial payments, each instalment creates a separate electronic document, but the customer invoice status is not updated until the invoice is fully paid.

The processing flow mirrors the customer invoice process with the electronic documents being updated with following statuses:

  1. Sending to Service Requested
  2. Sent to Service
  3. Acknowledged by Service — if the invoice is received successfully. The status updates to Rejected by Service when SAP Document and Reporting Compliance, cloud edition is unable to accept the electronic document — for example, when resubmitting a file that has already been acknowledged.
  4. Error at Service — if any error occurs while processing the electronic document for creation of CDAR_212.

Once the cloud edition acknowledges the electronic documents, the final XML format (CDAR_212) is generated and pushed back to ERP, and the electronic document status is updated to Accepted by the Service.

At this point, CDAR_212 is sent to both the seller and PPF. If both transmissions succeed and CDAR_212 is accepted by both recipient and PPF, the electronic document status is updated to Payment Received Status Sent.

If a failure occurs, it will be either a rejection or a technical error from PPF or the Receiver Platform (Buyer’s PA):

  • Technical error — the electronic document status is set to Error at Service. Use the Retry button in the eDocument Cockpit to reprocess.
  • Rejected by PPF — the electronic document status is set to Rejected by Public Portal and the PPF status description is Rejected by PPF. The document must be resubmitted.
  •  Rejected by Receiver Platform (Buyer’s PA) — the electronic document status is set to Rejected by Customer. The document must be resubmitted.

 

  Summary: CDAR Statuses

Sent by seller

Status

Code

Recipient

Registered

CDAR_200

PPF only

Payment Received

CDAR_212

PPF and Buyer

Completed

CDAR_209

Buyer only

 

Sent by buyer

Status

Code

Recipient

Accepted by Customer          

CDAR_205

Seller only

Partially Accepted by Customer

CDAR_206

Seller only

Disputed by Customer          

CDAR_207

Seller only

Suspended by Customer         

CDAR_208

Seller only

Rejected by Customer          

CDAR_210

PPF and Seller

Payment Sent by Customer      

CDAR_211

Seller only

▎ Note: If an invoice is Rejected by Receiving Platform (CDAR_213), this must also be reported to both PPF and the Seller.

entry.title: Re: June Developer Challenge - CAP Knowledge - Task 20 - Compiler messages
Student Exchange Opportunities Among SAP Academic Partnership Universities
entry.content:
Hello everyone!

An interesting question recently came up from a member of our academic community that we thought would be valuable to bring to the broader network for discussion 😊

Many universities are exploring ways to give students enrolled in SAP-related courses the chance to gain international academic experience through exchange programs or collaborations with other SAP partner institutions. We would love to hear how your institution approaches this topic.

Has your institution established exchange opportunities with other SAP partner universities? Are there scholarships, grants or university-to-university initiatives that help support these experiences? Have you found successful ways to build partnerships that enable student mobility or other international learning opportunities?

Whether your institution already has an established program, is currently exploring similar initiatives or simply has ideas and recommendations to share, your insights would be greatly appreciated!

We hope this discussion will serve as an opportunity to exchange ideas that could inspire new collaborations and expand international learning opportunities for students and professors across our SAP Academic Partnerships community.

entry.title: Turning Spreadsheet Logic into a Scalable Calculation Framework in SAP Analytics Cloud
entry.content:
Challenge:

We were working with a grid-like structure seen below, where 

  • Each row = Post ~ Shed Location (0–21)
  • Each column = Bay (0–28)
  • Data was available only at selected locations (shaded cells)
  • The remaining cells needed to be calculated

     

Across 4 compartments (51–54), this created hundreds of calculation points.

The real challenge was simple to ask, but hard to implement:

How do we calculate values for all empty cells using nearby available cells 
without writing and maintaining hundreds of formulas?

How it looked in Excel?

Let’s take one example from the sheet  : Cell M86Excel formula:  

=((J86/POWER(24,2)) + (N86/POWER(8,2)) + (M83/POWER(15,2)) + (M89/POWER(15,2))) / ((1/POWER(24,2)) + (1/POWER(8,2)) + (1/POWER(15,2)) + (1/POWER(15,2)))

What does this really mean? 

To calculate one cell, we take values from nearby cells, but closer ones influence the result more.

In this grid:

  • Each Bay = 8 metres
  • Each Post = 5 metres

So for M86:

Source

Distance

Impact

 

J86

24

Small

N86

8

Large

M83

15

Medium

M89

15

Medium

Then we:

  • Multiply each value by its weight
  • Add everything
  • Divide by total weight

This is essentially a distance-based weighted average

The problem with Excel:

While this works…

  • Every cell has a different formula
  • It quickly becomes hard to maintain
  • Not scalable across compartments
  • Impossible to reuse inside SAC directly

What we did in SAP Analytics Cloud?

Instead of copying formulas everywhere, we converted the logic into a data-driven model

 

Step 1: Store the logic as data (Config Table)

Instead of writing formulas, we created a config table.

Each row represents one target cell.

For example (for M86):

 For each cell, we store , for our example of cell M86 :

Target → Compartment = 52, Bay 4, Post 18

power 1 = 1/(24^2) = 0.0017361,

Source1 =Compartment = 52 Bay 1, Post 8

Power 2 = 1/(8^2) = 0.0156250

Source2 =Compartment = 52, Bay 5, Post 18

Power 3 =  1/(15^2) = 0.0044444

Source 3 :- Compartment =52,Bay =  4, Post =  21

Step 2:- Use ONE SAC Calculation

Now SAC runs a single reusable logic for all cells:

Result = (Value1 × Power1 + Value2 × Power2 + Value3 × Power3 + Value4 × Power4) / DenomAdd

  • No hardcoding 
  • No duplication 
  • Works for every compartment

Step 3 - Control the Calculation Flow

We added a simple control flag “StickyCardNotPlaced” in the Config dimension:

  • 3 → Base rows (input-driven rows)
  • 4 → Key columns (input-driven Cols)
  • 5 → Remaining cells

 This ensures:

  • Correct calculation order
  • No circular dependencies
  • Stable results

SAC Story (End Result)

Why this approach works

Instead of managing hundreds of formulas:

  •  We moved logic into data (config)
  •  Built one reusable calculation
  •  Made the model flexible and scalable
  •  Simplified maintenance and validation

Rather than replicating Excel formulas, we transformed the logic into a config-driven framework and let SAC handle the calculations dynamically.

This approach allowed us to turn a complex spreadsheet model into a scalable SAC solution.

Happy to connect if you’re working on similar grid-based or planning scenarios

entry.title: Re: How to Include/Exclude Time Types into Eligbility for...
entry.content: The easiest way to manage this type of scenario is to maintain a field in the job information panel for Bonus Plan. Then, when you have a change where the employee is not eligible for a reason such as military service or maternity leave, you can have a business rule to update this field to a value like LOA. From there when they are off leave, the business rule updates to the correct plan. Therefore, you exclude the use based on bonus plan, the same way some of my clients exclude employees on a Sales Commission plan from an annual performance bonus.

entry.title: Can you create a MILL_OC Combined Order with a single child order?
entry.content:
We are running S/4HANA version 2021 and we are in the steel structure industry.

SAP's Combined Orders work well for us.  Our CNC planning software sends SAP multiple planned orders via interface that are for a "nesting" and SAP's MILL_OC creates the combined order for the nesting and combines the first operation (the CNC cutting operation).

BUT, sometimes the "nesting" consists of only a single planned order. We would like to use our same interface/process but when MILL_OC tries to create a Combined Order with only one child (original) order we get error message MILL_OC 013 Select more than one order.  This message is yellow but it behaves as an error; not a warning.  It is "hardcoded" so we cannot change the attributes of this message.

I asked Joule and it said that it should be possible to create a Combined order with only a single child order.

Question - has anyone been able to create a Combined Order with only a single child (original) order?

Thank you!

entry.title: Context Aware Data Action Scoping
entry.content:
Overview

Data actions are a critical component of end-to-end planning workflows in SAP Analytics Cloud, supporting everything from seeding, to allocations, to complex calculations. Despite their importance, one lingering question has persisted: how can we optimize the data action to process only the minimum data required based on user actions?

Until now, users have not had a straightforward mechanism for discrete change tracking at the planning record level - no built-in way to identify which specific dimension members or data was actually changed in a version during a planning session. Progress has been made in related areas: the Planning Area concept significantly improved the ability to limit the scope of data brought into a Public Edit session (and thus processed by a data action), and scripted approaches using the onAfterDataEntryProcess table event have allowed developers to capture changed members and pass them to data actions via parameters (see The onAfterDataEntryProcess event in SAC).  While certainly helpful, these either do not address this core issue of data action processing, or do so in a way that requires custom development effort, carries some risk, and are not always transparent.

Without a native way to know exactly what changed, the result has been that data actions are often scoped more broadly than necessary, which can lead to longer run times and consumption of unnecessary resources. That changes with our Q3/2026 QRC release.

 

Introducing Change-Context Data Action Scoping

Change-context data action scoping builds on the new Data Filter parameter capability, also introduced in our Q3/2026 QRC release (see Improved Data Action scoping with the new Data Filter Parameter type for a full overview).  It allows the data action author to use a data filter to define specific dimension(s) along which to track user updates (such as Cost Center, Product, or Time). During runtime, the system automatically identifies which members within the designated filter dimensions have changed data in the target version (or another designated version), and those members then become the filter scope.  No custom scripting, no workarounds - just a simple configuration option which tells SAC to automatically scope data action processing to the dimension elements where data that was actually changed by the user.

 

Using Change-Context Scoping

Change tracking is easily enabled within the data action during definition of the data filter parameter. When adding a new parameter, the data action author selects two inputs within the configuration: the dimensions to track changed members on (for example, Cost Center, Time, or Product), and the version to inspect for changes (either a fixed version such as Budget 2026, or a reference to a version parameter such as the target version). These are configured within the Input section of the parameter definition by changing the Visibility setting to Private, after which the data action author can add the specific dimension filter(s) and the relevant version for change tracking. At runtime, SAP Analytics Cloud identifies which members of the dimension(s) have modified records in the reference version and uses those as the filter scope.

If multiple dimensions are selected within the filters, then the data action will run on the cartesian product of impacted members across the applicable dimensions.

Note: The change-context filter should be configured as a dedicated data filter, distinct from any filters and input controls used to filter the story data. Setting the data filter parameter type to Private, and then selecting the ”Auto-Determined by version changes” ensures it operates as the change-tracking filter. Once configured, as with other parameters, the change-context data filter needs to be applied to the filter section for the relevant data action steps where filtering is required.

The change context members accumulate across runs — subsequent executions will include members changed in earlier runs within the same version, and resets on version publish.

 

Figure 1: Parameter Definition

Figure 2: Parameter Assignment

The included screenshots illustrate a scenario with a data action that performs a currency conversion and has configured change-context tracking enabled on the EB_OPEX_CC (cost center) dimension.  When the user enters data changes on the Information Technology cost center (see below), the data action runtime parses the user updates and detects Information Technology as the only cost center dimension element that has modified records, and scopes the execution accordingly (as can be seen in the Job Monitor for the specific execution).

Figure 3: Data Action Execution

 

Figure 4: Job Monitor

Summary

Change-context data action scoping is a meaningful step forward in making planning workflows in SAP Analytics Cloud more intelligent and efficient. By equipping data actions with native awareness of user changes, calculations automatically target only what’s been modified—streamlining processing and boosting responsiveness.   Easily configured in the data action designer with just a dimension selection and reference version, it eliminates the need for custom scripting or other complex workarounds.

 
We’d love to hear how it works for you—please share your experiences and feedback in the comments below.

entry.title: Re: June Developer Challenge - CAP Knowledge - Task 19 - CDS source formatter
Re: June Developer Challenge - CAP Knowledge - Task 20 - Compiler messages
Re: Are What's New & RASD sufficient for tracking minor a...
entry.content:
Hello!

The content is filtered to highlight functional additions,  it is not a comprehensive change log. Minor behavioral changes, UI label adjustments, layout shifts, and silent bug fixes with side effects are routinely absent.

The RASD (Release Assessment and Scope Dependency) tool is designed to assess scope impact, it tells which solution packages, business processes, and integration points are touched by a given release or HFC. It is valuable for identifying whether a particular process area is in scope for change, but it does not drill down to screen-level UI changes or low-level behavioral corrections. It will tell "ATP availability check is impacted," but it will not tell that the selection screen layout changed.

SAP Note 3646210 capture the actual development asset changes, including corrections to individual programs, selection screens, UI elements, and minor behavioral fixes that would never appear in the What's New Viewer.

Practical recommendation is to use the three layers in combination:

  • RASD - to identify which process areas are in scope
  • What's New Viewer - to understand functional enhancements that require key user training or configuration decisions
  • SAP Note PDFs (3646210 and HFC-specific notes) - for final operational gate-check, specifically looking for any changes in process-critical transactions and apps

Preliminary What's New content covers net-new features or major enhancements. A change to the selection screen layout or field arrangement on the ATP check for Production/Planned Orders is a correction or minor behavioral update, not a new feature.

Would it appear in Product Assistance (SAP Help Portal)? If the screen change was significant that the field-level documentation needed updating, the relevant help topic may reflect the new state. However, the Help Portal does not publish change logs at the level of "in HFC12, field X moved to position Y." It documents the current state, not the delta. So it is useful for understanding what the screen now looks like, but not for discovering that it changed or why.

RASD would flag that the ATP / availability check area was touched in HFC12. But RASD alone would not tell the nature of the change at the UI level.

The most likely place to find this specific change documented is in the detailed correction/change list PDF attached to the HFC12-specific SAP Note (a child or successor note under the 3646210 umbrella, or a dedicated HFC12 delivery note).

For changes that can’t be founded documented anywhere, raise a SAP incident referencing the observed change and if something is missing, support can point you to the correct note or confirm the change was intentional.

The Expert Search (available at support.sap.com under SAP for Me) is a more powerful alternative to the standard SAP Note search. Unlike the basic search, it allows you to filter notes by component, release status, category, priority, and date range simultaneously, making it well suited for systematically monitoring release and upgrade-related documentation.

The standard search on SAP Note 3646210 only shows you that one note. The Expert Search lets you cast a wider net,  for example, finding all release-related notes across a component area (such as PP or MM) that were published after a specific HFC date. This helps surface related notes, known restrictions, and how-to guides that are referenced by, but not attached to, the central HFC note.

Setting up automatic notifications. Rather than running this search manually after each HFC, the Expert Search supports saved searches with email alerts. KBA 2771695 (How to get notifications about new SAP Notes and KBAs) explains how to configure this. Setting up a notification on SAP Note 3646210 or on the XX-SER-REL-S4HANA component means your team is alerted automatically when new HFC PDFs are attached.

entry.title: Re: Business Partner Migration from R3 into S4 Hana publi...
entry.content:
Hello!

In S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, the BP-Customer-Supplier number relationship is governed by the CVI (Customer-Vendor Integration) framework. Deactivating "Same Numbers" is the correct starting point, but it is not sufficient on its own to produce three different numbers on one BP.

The Migration Cockpit's default behavior overrides flexible grouping. When using the Migration Cockpit, the BP creation logic for Customer and Supplier objects defaults to same-number assignment regardless of SSCUI settings, unless the number range and grouping configuration is explicitly set up to force external number assignment for KUNNR and LIFNR independently. The Cockpit creates the BP first, then synchronizes and the sync logic uses the BP number as the default for both KUNNR and LIFNR unless a different number range interval is active and mapped.

Grouping assignment at the BP level determines which number range is used for KUNNR/LIFNR. Even with "Same Numbers" off, if the BP grouping assigned during creation maps to a number range that overlaps with or defaults to the BP number range, will still get same-number output. You need distinct, non-overlapping number range intervals for BP, Customer, and Supplier  and the BP grouping must explicitly point to the Customer/Supplier number ranges separately.

Here is the configuration and sequencing that should produce BP 50001 / KUNNR 123 / LIFNR 456, configuration prerequisites (before any migration):

Step 1. Number Range Setup - define three completely separate, non-overlapping number range intervals:

  • BP internal number range (e.g. 50000–59999, internal)
  • Customer external number range covering your legacy KUNNR values
  • Supplier external number range covering your legacy LIFNR values

The Customer and Supplier ranges must be defined as external (not internal) so the Migration Cockpit can pass the legacy numbers directly.

Step 2.  BP Grouping - create or configure BP groupings:

  • The grouping used for migrated legacy BPs maps to the internal BP number range
  • The Customer account group maps to the external Customer number range
  • The Vendor account group maps to the external Supplier number range
  • The BP grouping and the Customer/Supplier account groups must be independently mapped in the CVI configuration

 

Step 3.   Confirm "Same Numbers" is deactivated in both. Also verify that the number range synchronization setting (separate from "Same Numbers") is not inadvertently forcing BP number reuse.

 

For dual-role entities (one BP with both KUNNR and LIFNR):

  • Migrate Customer first via Migration Cockpit object "Customer",  this creates the BP with an internal BP number and assigns the legacy KUNNR as an external Customer number
  • Extend with Supplier role using the "Supplier - extend existing record by new org levels" approach, passing the legacy LIFNR as the external Supplier number against the already-created BP

When extending with the Supplier role, the Migration Cockpit or API must receive the existing BP number as the anchor, and the legacy LIFNR as the external number for the Supplier account group. If instead the tool tries to create a new BP, it will produce same-number behavior.

The extension step is not creating a Supplier role on the existing BP, it is creating a new BP with a new Supplier record and assigning the same number to both. Check in the Migration Cockpit log whether the Supplier step is referencing the existing BP GUID or creating a new BP object. If it is creating a new one, the configuration linkage between the Customer BP and the Supplier extension is not set ok.

SAP Note 2748819 covers the CVI number assignment logic, and KBA 3393756 covers the extension approach.

CVI_EI011 / CVI_EI041 errors occur during BP-Customer/Supplier synchronization when the CVI framework can’t resolve the account group mapping or finds a number range conflict. At 100k+ records, even a low error rate produces a large absolute number of failed records. Mitigate by running a small batch (500–1000 records) with the full configuration in place before bulk migration, and analyzing the CVI sync log.

CVIV_UI 6 "Enter External Number": triggered when the system expects an external number but none is provided, or when the external number falls outside the configured range interval. At scale this typically surfaces as a batch failure rather than an interactive prompt. Ensure every Customer and Supplier record in migration file carries the legacy number.

 

Customer-first plus Supplier extension is the correct architectural approach for dual-role entities. To keep the two numbers distinct without special interface logic:

  • The Supplier extension must use a different account group than the Customer, mapped to a different number range this is what tells the CVI framework to assign a new external number rather than reuse the existing one
  • The extension process must pass the legacy LIFNR explicitly as the external number for the Supplier account group, if it is left blank, the system will either error or default to same-number

 

Additional SAP Notes:

SAP Note 2265093 — CVI: Customer/Vendor Integration general setup

https://help.sap.com/docs/s4hana-best-practices/implementation-of-sap-s-4hana-cloud-private-edition-2023-with-sap-best-practices-setting-up-merged-client-b8b90771a40d487db747181eedbd573e/business-partner-customer-vendor-number-range-settings?locale=en-US

entry.title: Introducing Support Content — the additional Customer Support knowledge repository
entry.content:
Support Content is a curated knowledge hub that explains SAP systems from the perspective of Customer Support experts. Built from real Customer Support experience, it documents product logic, troubleshooting approaches, and recommended practices — helping you resolve issues faster and understand system behavior.

What you'll find there

Support Content provides practical information about SAP products and scenarios, including:

  • Explanations of system logic and expected behavior
  • Business process guidance and context
  • Step‑by‑step troubleshooting guides
  • Common configuration scenarios
  • Frequently asked questions (FAQs)
  • White papers and architecture examples
  • Best practices and recommendations

Content covers a broad range of SAP offerings — from newer topics such as SAP Analytics Cloud and SAP HANA Smart Data Integration to long‑standing products such as SAP Crystal Reports, SAP Datasphere, and SAP Extended Warehouse Management (EWM) — and everything that falls under Customer Support’s scope.

How it’s used and maintained

Support Content is designed to be a practical reference for customers. Entries are linked to other SAP repositories and are frequently referenced to speed diagnosis and resolution. The repository is reviewed and updated over time, with new topics added according to demand and evolving product landscapes.

How can you share feedback?

Feedback is welcome and helps the repository stay accurate and useful. Use the platform’s comments feature on each page to:

  • Report unclear or out‑of‑date information
  • Suggest additional scenarios or examples
  • Reference related support cases or product versions for context

You have two options to help us to improve the content:

  • Feedback (anonymous): using the Feedback feature on the right-hand side of the page, where you can tell us how helpful the content is and add an optional text.
  • Comment (need an authenticated user): you can provide your comments and be specific on which piece of the text you are referring to.
    First, select the words and click on the button:

    Then, you can add your comment:

* The Comment option will be gradually introduced on the topics. 

When commenting, include a brief description of the issue or the suggested change to help reviewers act more quickly.

Why use Support Content

  • Saves time by providing authoritative explanations and troubleshooting steps
  • Helps you understand root causes and expected system behavior
  • Complements other SAP knowledge sources by focusing on practical, support‑driven guidance

 Explore the Support Content to find product guidance, scenario examples, troubleshooting resources, and much more, tailored to help with your support needs and make the most of your SAP landscape.

Want to know where Support Content originates? Look at this blog post.

Cheers!!
Marcelo Pinheiro
Support Content Business Owner

entry.title: Re: June Developer Challenge - CAP Knowledge - Task 20 - Compiler messages
Re: Manage Customer Line Items – Net Days and WBS Element...
entry.content:
Hello!

The Net Days field in Manage Customer Line Items most probably  reads from PENDAYS (the stored field), not from VERZN (the dynamic calculation). Since PENDAYS is only populated when the payment program runs penalty charge calculation, the field shows 0 for all normal invoices.

Use the Due Date (Fe.vencimiento) and Days of Delay (Días de demora) columns instead, both visible in your screenshot and both reliably populated. For the Days of Delay column (Días de demora) it shows the number of days past due calculated relative to today's date. In screenshot this column shows -52 and -22 for various items. This is the field to use for overdue analysis, not Net Days.

 

KBA 3499711 directly addresses the WBS Element empty behavior for Public Edition.

Cause - there are two different views of journal entry data:

  • Ledger View: reflects backend table ACDOCA
  • Entry View: reflects backend table BSEG

The Display Line Items reflects the Ledger View (ACDOCA). The WBS Element field is blank in ACDOCA for certain document types, specifically customer invoice line items where the WBS assignment is stored in the Entry View (BSEG) but not carried through to the Ledger View. Use the Display Line Item Entry app to see WBS Element on customer invoice line items.

entry.title: Attendance and Absence Collision Groupings – A Game Changer in SAP SuccessFactors Time Management
entry.content:
Introduction:

One of my clients had a real business pain point where employees who work on a annual leave or any leave for that matter was unable to record the overtime in the Timesheet. It was due to the time collision that was restricting them to apply for the attendance which is Overtime. They had to manually keep a track in the excel sheet , compute and pay them Overtime.

How Attendance and Absence Collision Groupings came to the rescue!

Attendance and Absence Collision Groupings was introduced as part of the 1H 2026 Release which was one of the much awaited and highlighted features.To make the section visible , make sure the TLMTriggerTasksJob is scheduled in the System and is run. You can monitor the job from the Scheduled Job Manager

However if the job is not scheduled , it has to scheduled from the provisioning.

Next up is RBP, RBP needs to be provided to make the section visible .

 

Configurations to handle Attendance and Absence Groupings:

Step 1 : Create Time Collision Groupings 

Manage Data -> Create New -> Time Collision Grouping -> Category : Attendance

Step 2 : Create Time Collision Groupings 

Manage Data -> Create New -> Time Collision Grouping -> Category : Absence

Step 3 : Assigning the Groupings to the Time Type

Manage data -> Search -> Time Type -> Assign the Grouping to the Collision Grouping field

Step 4 : Create Time Management Collision Resolution 

Manage Data -> Create New -> Time  Management Collision Resolution 

Make sure to add the incoming and Existing Grouping are added vice versa.Decide on whether to show / hide the collision resolution message.I will advice it to hide so that the user doenst get confused mistaking it for error.

Collision Resolution Action - Allow Concurrent :

Will allow incoming entries to co-exist during the same timings with the existing grouping

Collision Resolution Action - Allow outside Absence Hours :

Will allow incoming entries to exist only outside the absence hours with the existing grouping

Business Benefits

Using Attendance and Absence Collision Groupings offers several advantages:

  • Eliminates manual overtime tracking in spreadsheets.
  • Reduces payroll processing effort and manual calculations.
  • Provides greater flexibility for organizations with unique attendance policies.
  • Improves data accuracy by allowing valid attendance and absence records to coexist.
  • Enhances the employee and administrator experience by reducing unnecessary collision errors.

    Conclusion :

    Organizations that previously relied on manual workarounds for scenarios such as overtime during leave can now configure the system to support these business requirements directly. With the right collision grouping and resolution settings, administrators gain greater control over how attendance and absence records interact, reducing administrative effort while ensuring accurate payroll processing.

entry.title: Re: June Developer Challenge - CAP Knowledge - Task 19 - CDS source formatter
SAP Event Mesh for Beginners – Part 1: Event-Driven Architecture, Topics, Queues and Initial Setup
entry.content:
SAP Event Mesh — Part 1: Event-Driven Architecture & Setup

*A 3-part hands-on series on SAP Event Mesh: Event-Driven Architecture, Pub-Sub, and CPI integration.*

> **Series at a glance**
> - **Part 1 (this post):** EDA concepts + provisioning the Event Mesh instance, queues, subscriptions, and service key
> - **Part 2:** Testing with Postman + CPI iFlow configuration (publish & consume)
> - **Part 3:** Common errors, fixes, and a quick-reference cheat sheet
>
> **Environment used in this series:** SAP BTP — AP11 (Asia Pacific) · Namespace `S4HC/Testing/Masterdata` · March 2026

---

## Why this series

Most SAP integration developers learn point-to-point patterns first — RFC, SOAP, OData. They work, but they tightly couple systems together. The moment you need a *second* system to react to the same business event, you end up duplicating calls and adding brittle dependencies.

SAP Event Mesh flips that model. In this first post we'll cover the architectural concepts you need, then walk through provisioning Event Mesh end to end so it's ready for the integration work in Part 2.

---

## 1. Event-Driven Architecture (EDA) — Core Concepts

### 1.1 What is Event-Driven Architecture?

Event-Driven Architecture (EDA) is a software design pattern in which systems communicate by **producing and consuming events**. Instead of calling each other directly (RFC, SOAP, OData), systems publish events to a central hub, and any interested system subscribes to those events.

Why EDA over traditional integration:

- **Loose coupling** — systems don't need to know about each other
- **Scalability** — multiple subscribers can consume the same event
- **Resilience** — if a subscriber is down, events wait safely in the queue
- **Real-time processing** — events are handled as they happen

### 1.2 How Event Mesh works — the Pub-Sub flow

```
   PUBLISHER                 SAP EVENT MESH                  SUBSCRIBERS
 ─────────────            ────────────────────            ────────────────
                          TOPICS
 S/4HANA Cloud  ── publish ──►  CustomerMaster
 (Event Source)                 SupplierMaster
                                     │
                                routed to
                                     ▼
                          QUEUES
                          Q: shopify   ── subscribe ──►  Shopify
                          Q: c4c       ── subscribe ──►  C4C / SAP Sales
                          Q: 3pl       ── subscribe ──►  3PL System
                                                         DMS / Archive
```

**Flow explained:**

1. S/4HANA Cloud (the **publisher**) sends an event to a **topic** (e.g. `CustomerMaster`).
2. Event Mesh receives the event and routes it to every **queue** subscribed to that topic.
3. Each subscriber system (Shopify, C4C, 3PL) has its own queue.
4. Consumer systems pull messages from their own queue, independently of each other.

The key insight: one publish can fan out to many consumers, and each consumer reads at its own pace.

### 1.3 The three types of events

| Event Type | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Notification Event** | A lightweight signal that something happened. No payload data. | Business Partner created in S/4HANA |
| **Data Event** | Carries the actual changed data payload. | Customer Master record with all fields |
| **Transaction Event** | Represents a complete business transaction. | Sales Order confirmed with all line items |

### 1.4 Key terminology

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Topic** | A named channel where publishers send events. All queues subscribed to a topic receive the event. Topics follow the namespace pattern. |
| **Queue** | A message store for a specific subscriber. Each consumer system (Shopify, C4C…) gets its own queue. |
| **Namespace** | A prefix that groups topics and queues. Example: `S4HC/Testing/Masterdata`. All topics must follow the namespace rules. |
| **Publisher** | The system that sends events (e.g. S/4HANA Cloud, a CPI iFlow). |
| **Subscriber / Consumer** | The system that receives events by listening to a queue. |
| **Pub-Hub Model** | SAP's term for the central Event Mesh hub through which all messages pass. |
| **EMIS** | Event Mesh Integration Suite — the embedded Event Mesh inside SAP Integration Suite. Same concept, different access path. |

---

## 2. SAP Event Mesh — Setup Steps

> **EMIS vs. standalone:** EMIS (Event Mesh Integration Suite) is the embedded version inside SAP Integration Suite. It behaves the same as standalone Event Mesh — you just access it through the Integration Suite client.

### Step 1 — Create the Event Mesh instance in your sub-account

1. In **SAP BTP Cockpit**, navigate to your sub-account.
2. Go to **Services → Service Marketplace** and search for **Event Mesh**.
3. Create a new instance with a service descriptor like this:

```json
{
  "resources": { "units": "10" },
  "options": { "management": true, "messagingrest": true, "messaging": true },
  "rules": {
    "topicRules": {
      "publishFilter":   ["${namespace}/*"],
      "subscribeFilter": ["${namespace}/*"]
    },
    "queueRules": {
      "publishFilter":   ["${namespace}/*"],
      "subscribeFilter": ["${namespace}/*"]
    }
  },
  "version": "1.1.0",
  "emname": "S4_RudhTesting",
  "namespace": "S4HC/Testing/Masterdata"
}
```

> **Tip:** The namespace `S4HC/Testing/Masterdata` means **all** topics and queues must start with this prefix. The `${namespace}/*` filters allow any topic or queue under that namespace.

### Step 2 — Create queues

Each consuming system needs its own queue. In the Event Mesh dashboard:

1. Open the Event Mesh dashboard (`S4_RudhTesting`).
2. Go to the **Queues** tab.
3. Click **Create Queue** for each consumer:
   - `S4HC/Testing/Masterdata/Shopify` — for Shopify integration
   - `S4HC/Testing/Masterdata/C4C` — for C4C / SAP Sales Cloud
   - `S4HC/Testing/Masterdata/3pl` — for the 3PL / logistics system

> **Note:** Queue names must start with your namespace prefix. Each queue is independent — one message published to a topic lands in **all** subscribed queues.

### Step 3 — Subscribe queues to topics

Once the queues exist, subscribe each one to the relevant topic(s):

1. Click a queue → click the subscription icon → **Queue Subscriptions**.
2. Enter the topic name to subscribe to. For example:
   - Queue `…/Shopify` → Topic `S4HC/Testing/Masterdata/Shopify/CustomerMaster`
   - Queue `…/C4C` → Topic `S4HC/Testing/Masterdata/C4C/CustomerMaster`

> **Topic names are CASE-SENSITIVE.** `CustomerMaster` and `customermaster` are different topics. Always match the exact case you configured. (This bites people later in CPI — more on that in Part 3.)

### Step 4 — Create a service key

The service key holds the credentials (OAuth2 client ID, secret, token endpoint, and API URIs) external apps use to connect to Event Mesh.

1. In BTP Cockpit, open your Event Mesh service instance.
2. Click **Create Service Key** and give it a name.
3. Copy / download the JSON — this is your connection credential.

**Service key — the fields that matter:**

| Field | What it's for |
|---|---|
| `namespace` | Your event namespace prefix: `S4HC/Testing/Masterdata` |
| `management.uri` | Hub backend URL for managing queues via REST API |
| `management.oa2.tokenendpoint` | OAuth2 token URL for management-API authentication |
| `messaging[httprest].uri` | REST endpoint for publishing messages via HTTP POST |
| `messaging[amqp10ws].uri` | WebSocket endpoint for AMQP connections (used in CPI) |
| `uaa.clientid` | OAuth2 Client ID — used in all authentication calls |
| `uaa.clientsecret` | OAuth2 Client Secret — **keep this secret; rotate immediately if exposed** |

> **Security note for anyone publishing this:** Never paste a real `clientsecret`, `clientid`, or your tenant-specific token-endpoint subdomain into a public post. Treat the service key like a password. In all examples in this series, those values are placeholders.

---

## What's next

You now have a provisioned Event Mesh instance, queues bound to topics, and a service key with the credentials you'll need. In **Part 2**, we'll prove the setup works with Postman, then wire up the real integration in CPI — an HTTP iFlow to publish and an AMQP iFlow to consume.

*Found this useful? Part 2 covers the hands-on CPI integration, and Part 3 is a troubleshooting cheat sheet built from real errors.*

---

*Tags: SAP Event Mesh, Event-Driven Architecture, SAP BTP, Integration Suite, Pub-Sub, S/4HANA Cloud*

entry.title: Re: Customer Returns Delivery (A2X) API is Not long text
entry.content:
Dear @liqi_2bdb12,

The Customer Returns Delivery (A2X) API does not provide a separate API object or field named Long Text. SAP documents delivery text handling for this API under the generic delivery text operations, such as Outbound Delivery Header Text and Outbound Delivery Item Text, not under a separate Customer Returns Delivery Long Text node. The technical entities used in SAP Help examples are A_OutbDeliveryHeaderText and A_OutbDeliveryItemText and the text content itself is handled in the TextElementText field

More details below:

SAP Help – Customer Returns Delivery (A2X)
SAP Help – Operations for Customer Returns Delivery API
SAP Help – Change Outbound Delivery Header Text
SAP Help – Change Outbound Delivery Item Text

If the required text is maintained on the preceding customer return order rather than on the returns delivery, then this is a different business object and the relevant API to review is Customer Return (A2X)

More details below:

SAP Help – Customer Return (A2X, OData V2)

If the required long text cannot be accessed through the released API entities available in the customer tenant then there is no separate released CDS/API object in Public Cloud for this requirement. In that case the requirement should be submitted to SAP as an improvement request through SAP Customer Influence

More details below:

SAP KBA 2963059 – How to Submit Feature Requests for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: Outside of Simulation in pMRP
entry.content:
@RaghavanKathamuthu 

I think "Outside of simulation" is related to the In-house Production Time maintained in the Material Master. The system calculates the production capacity based on the available working days and the maintained production time. For example, if the In-house Production Time is 8 days and the total order quantity is 12,000, then the calculation would be:

  • Working days in the month = 22
  • Daily production = 12,000 / 22 = 545.45
  • Production for 8 days = 545.45 × 8 = 4,363.6

I believe this calculated value is why the system shows "Outside of simulation." Please correct me if my understanding is incorrect.

entry.title: Re: Incoterms based on Ship-to Party
entry.content:
Dear @SiddheshParab,

In standard Public Cloud, Incoterms in sales documents are determined from the Sold-to Party, not from the Ship-to Party. SAP confirms that this is standard logic because Incoterms are considered part of the sales agreement with the Sold-to Party, while the Ship-to Party is only the goods recipient. This cannot be changed through standard customizing

BUT

For this requirement the supported approach in is to use Custom Logic with the released Sales Document BAdIs. SAP confirms that Incoterms can be set or changed by implementing custom logic in the following BAdIs:

More details below:

SAP KBA 3152657 – Use Custom Logic to Set Incoterms in Sales Documents

Header level:
SD_SLS_MODIFY_HEAD_BUSINESS

Item level:
SD_SLS_MODIFY_ITEM_BUSINESS

The custom logic can read the Ship-to Party partner function WE, read the corresponding Ship-to Party sales area data, for example from I_CustomerSalesArea and then fill the required Incoterms fields in the sales document

More details below:

SAP Help – Business Add-Ins for Sales Documents

Transportation Management is not required for this approach. The requirement is handled in SD sales document custom logic, not in TM

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: F3041 - Import supplier invoices - PO not released error
entry.content:
Dear @s-tashev_80,

SAP confirms that purchase-order-based invoices are not supported in this app. SAP KBA 3443682 also confirms that there is no purchase-order-relevant column in the F3041 templat

More details below:

SAP Help Import Supplier Invoices

SAP KBA 3443682 - Can't import purchase-order-based invoices in app "Import Supplier Invoices"

Therefore the PO itself is probably not the root cause. The likely issue is that the uploaded invoice draft/worklist or the Excel file is still indirectly carrying old or incorrect reference data

Please check the following:

1. SAP confirms that entries can remain in the F3041 worklist and need to be posted or deleted before a clean upload/reprocessing

More details below:

SAP KBA 3493978 - “Clear your worklist first. Either post the invoices in the worklist or delete them” error in Import Supplier Invoices (F3041) App

2. Do not reuse an old template or a manually modified/copied file

3. SAP confirms that hidden template fields exist in the F3041 template. Hidden columns are still technically part of the upload, so any hidden or copied value should be checked

More details below:

SAP KBA 3363741 - Unhide hidden template fields of Import Supplier Invoices.

4. Check visible cells, hidden columns, hidden rows, filters, copied ranges, reference fields, document header text, item text, assignment/reference fields and any old data left from a previous upload

5. If the system still reaches error then the PO status should be checked in Flexible Workflow Administration. SAP KBA 3561911 describes cases where supplier invoice processing can show a purchasing document not yet released error when the PO release/approval status is not synchronized

More details below:

SAP KBA 3561911 - Error:'Purchasing document <XXX> not yet released' or Purchase order status is' In approval' but approval status is 'Released'

If the requirement is to upload PO-based supplier invoices in bulk then F3041 is not the correct standard app. SAP confirms that there is no standard Excel template for bulk upload of PO-based supplier invoices and the standard option for bulk PO-based creation is the supplier invoice API

More details below:

SAP KBA 3769897 - No standard template to upload bulk purchase order-based invoices in create supplier invoice - advanced (MIRO)

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: From Prototype to Production with Joule Studio: Recap of Session 4 of Architecting the AE Webinar
entry.content:
The Enterprise Readiness Gap

If you are using Claude Code, Cursor, or any modern agentic development environment, you can build a working agent in hours. It will look impressive. It will handle the use case you designed it for. And it will have almost nothing in common with what an enterprise deployment actually requires.

Getting an AI agent to production in an enterprise context means: aligning business stakeholders, IT, and architecture before a single line of code is written. It means authentication, role-based access control, and single sign-on handled correctly. It means enterprise-grade infrastructure that scales, testing that covers edge cases and evaluates actual agent behavior, audit trails that satisfy compliance requirements, and monitoring that continues long after go-live.

The gap between a prototype and an enterprise-ready agent is not a matter of effort. It is a structural difference in what the two things are built for.

That gap is what I called 'The Enterprise Readiness Gap', and it is precisely what Joule Studio is designed to close.

SAP's positioning is straightforward: Joule Studio is the complete environment to build, run, deploy, manage, and govern agentic AI solutions at enterprise scale.

 

The right to win rests on three capabilities that are difficult to replicate outside of SAP's ecosystem. First, an intent-based development environment that supports everything from natural language vibe coding to full pro-code with your own IDEs and frameworks. Second, business context built directly into both design and runtime: SAP Knowledge Graph, Signavio, LeanIX, Cloud ALM, domain models, and backend systems connected through MCP servers. Third, a trusted governance layer where observability, logging, audit, and lifecycle management are built into the platform, not bolted on after the fact.

Where Joule Studio Sits

Joule Studio is the development pillar inside Joule Work, SAP's new engagement layer. Joule Work provides three surfaces: Conversations, Spaces, and Development. Joule Studio is the Development surface, accessible via browser, desktop, or mobile. The architecture brings together a design-time environment, an SAP-managed runtime, and governance connections to LeanIX, Cloud ALM, and Signavio for agent evaluations, tracing, and performance monitoring.

Three builders are available in the design-time environment today: an Agent Builder for Python-based conversational agents, a Workflow Builder powered by an SAP-n8n partnership, and a CAP App Builder for UI5-based applications. Each produces standard, portable, Git-versionable output, not proprietary artifacts.

Joule Studio Architecture

 For teams who want to work in familiar tooling, VS Code, Claude Code, and Cursor can all connect into the same Joule Studio project. There is no migration, no copy-paste handover. You work in the web editor when you want speed, switch to your preferred IDE when you want depth, and everything stays in sync.

You can get access to all the session recordings and slides by registering here: https://events.sap.com/eaa-autent-web/en_us/home.html

The Six-Phase Lifecycle: From Intent to Production

Julia Kloppenburg walked us through the complete intent-driven development lifecycle, end to end, and then demonstrated it live on a working agent. The six phases represent a structured approach to close enterprise readiness gap.

Phase 1: Intent. Everything starts with a business outcome described in natural language. What needs to change? Who is affected? What does success look like? Joule Studio captures this as a durable problem statement, enriches it with real enterprise context, from Signavio process maps to LeanIX architecture data to SAP Knowledge Graph semantics, and carries it forward through the entire lifecycle. The intent does not get lost in the handover between business and IT. It becomes the thread that connects every subsequent phase.

Phase 2: Product Requirements Document. From the grounded intent, Joule Studio generates a full PRD: problem statement, target users, success measures, requirements grouped by priority, user stories, acceptance criteria, solution architecture, and milestones. What makes this different from a standard requirements exercise is that the requirements reflect how the business actually runs, grounded in real process and architecture data rather than theoretical assumptions. This phase also forces an early decision: activate standard SAP capabilities, configure what already exists, or build something new.

Phase 3: Specification. The PRD becomes an implementation-ready blueprint. Scope, architecture, integrations, security, task breakdown, business object definitions, and system contracts are all defined here. Critically, compliance and policy requirements are embedded at this stage. Safety is built in, not applied after the fact. This is the point where technical choices and integration patterns are locked down, which eliminates the rework loops that typically consume later phases of development.

Phase 4: Code Generation. The build agent generates agents, workflows, and CAP applications directly from the specification, with SAP context integrated throughout: the right domain models, the right integration approach, N8N where orchestration is part of the solution. The full code base is exposed, rewritable, and Git-versionable. Developers own the output. Teams can inspect it, refactor it, and apply their normal engineering discipline to it.

Phase 5: Testing. Joule Studio validates the end-to-end flow, not isolated components. Automated evaluations assess whether the agent behaved as intended, not just whether it ran. A single test report provides coverage, behavior checks, and per-component failure signals. This is the checkpoint that makes the deployment decision a confident one rather than an optimistic one.

Phase 6: Deployment and Operations. The full solution is pushed to GitHub and deployed to SAP-managed runtime. SAP handles provisioning, scaling, monitoring, and day-to-day operations. Cost and usage signals are tracked continuously in production. The AI Agent Hub serves as the central governance control plane: discover agents, manage their lifecycle, observe behavior, trace decisions, and respond to issues. Deployment is not the end of the story. It is where the improvement loop begins.

Julia demonstrated the full lifecycle on a real system, not a recording, using a new hire onboarding agent built entirely in Joule Studio.

The use case is familiar to anyone who has joined a new organisation: navigating tasks, finding documents, completing mandatory training, and getting answers to questions without continuously interrupting HR teams. The agent handles all of that conversationally, surfaces relevant documents on demand, tracks onboarding task completion, and escalates unresolved queries to HR via notification.

The demo started with the intent phase, where Joule Studio generated an intent fit score of 88 percent from a name and a short description. It showed the auto-generated problem statement, goals, and metrics, with the Signavio recruit-to-retire process visible as business context. The PRD contained detailed requirements, user stories, and solution architecture. The specification included MCP integration details and agent behavior definitions. The test suite covered structural, server, and agent tests, all of which passed. The deployment view showed the agent live, connected to SAP SuccessFactors via Agent-to-Agent protocol, running on SAP BTP.

The point of the demo was not the use case itself. It was the coherence of the process: a single environment that carries the intent from natural language through to a deployed, tested, governed production agent, with the business context embedded throughout.

 Key Clarifications

Joule Studio and SAP Build are not the same thing, and Build is not being replaced. SAP Build continues unchanged: customer-managed, supported. Joule Studio is SAP-managed, AI-first, and designed for intent-driven agentic development. 

What you can build: Conversational agents, system-triggered agents, deterministic CAP applications, N8N workflows, and multi-agent systems. The platform is not limited to agentic use cases.

What Comes Next

Session 5 on July 1 turns to the question of how SAP's Innovation Factory approaches architecting and activating AI at the enterprise level with Helmut Grimm, Raghu Banda, Jose Augusto Tornaro, and Vishal Mehta to unpack the methodology behind moving from architecture planning to active AI deployment.

If Session 4 answered how Joule Studio works, Session 5 addresses how the broader activation journey is structured across an enterprise engagement.

You can get access to all the session recordings and slides by registering here: https://events.sap.com/eaa-autent-web/en_us/home.html

entry.title: Re: Service Entries substitution
entry.content:
Hello!

When a substitution is activated in the My Inbox settings, the behavior for already existing work items depends on the substitution type configured:

  • Active Substitution (Take Over): The substitutor takes over the inbox, existing work items that were already in the original person's inbox at the moment substitution is activated are not automatically transferred to the substitutor. They remain assigned to the original recipient.
  • Passive Substitution (Forward): New items are forwarded to the substitutor going forward, but existing items stay with the original person.

The timeline of "SE returned without clear reason" correlating with a manager activating substitution, it is the workflow engine's response to finding a work item assigned to someone who has delegated their inbox after the item was already delivered. Check the workflow instance log for the returned SE items, look at the timestamp of the return action and compare it to when the manager activated substitution.

Do forwarded items also appear in the substitutor's inbox?

  • New SE created after substitution is activated: appears in substitutor inbox; forwarded automatically to substitutor
  • SE already in manager's inbox when substitution activated: remains with original recipient, not automatically forwarded in substitutor inbox
  • Manager manually forwards an existing item to substitutor: appears in substitutor inbox; but requires manual action by manager

Your managers need to be instructed to manually forward pending SE work items to their substitutor before activating substitution, not after.

Is cost controller setup (review + forward, no approve) aligned with SAP Best practices?

  • Cost controller = Responsible Person on WO, receives SE in inbox for review/comment/reject
  • Cost controller forwards to Manager for approval
  • Cost controller does not have SE approval authorization
  • Single approval step (Manager)

Using the Responsible Person determination to involve a cost controller in the review step without giving them approval authority is a supported design. It keeps the approval authority clearly with the manager while giving the cost controller visibility.

The approach where the cost controller manually forwards the SE to the manager - SAP's best practice for multi-person involvement in SE processing is to use configured workflow steps rather than manual forwarding. A configured two-step workflow where step 1 is a "Review" task for the cost controller (with Reject and Forward/Approve options) and Step 2 is an "Approve" task for the manager. The cost controller's step can be configured with a short SLA and automatic escalation. If business decision is to avoid the second configured step, the manual forward approach can work, but you need to add a procedural control: cost controllers must add a mandatory comment when forwarding, and managers must be trained on the substitution sequencing issue described in above.
